column6:
Employers discriminating based on factors like race, sex, religion, and sexual orientation puts a limitation on freedom and free markets.

In order to preserve freedom, one must make an effort to not only protect our freedoms from the government, but also protect our freedoms from private citizens. Murder for example limits another's freedom to live and because of that deserves to be a crime. Employee discrimination also severely limits the freedoms of underprivileged classes while providing no tangible benefit to employers. Why should the rights of the employer supersede the rights of the employee when limiting an employer's right to discriminate causes them no harm?

 Worse yet, allowing employee discrimination is an impediment to the free market. Societies with widespread racism, sexism, homophobia etc. etc. put artificial limitations on human capital. When people are discouraged from working because they belong to a certain class, we limit our abilities to truly hire the person with the most merit.

"I completely disagree with @intense-use's stance on this. Just because someone's actions may not be immediately apparent doesn't mean they don't have a negative impact. We can't just dismiss the harm caused by discriminatory behavior because it's 'obvious' to us. What about people who are being discriminated against and can't see the harm? We need to consider their perspective too. And by the way, @intense-use, your argument that limiting employee discrimination is an impediment to the free market is flawed. It's not about limiting the rights of the employer, it's about ensuring that everyone has an equal chance to succeed, regardless of their background. We can't just let discriminatory practices run amok because it's 'good for business'."

"Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @intense-use and @soft-editor, please remember to engage in constructive dialogue. @blocking-sandstone, please provide more context or evidence to support your claims. Let's maintain a respectful and informative discussion."

column6:
>Success is literally defined as achieving the desired outcome, so yes, we have to factor in the inherently subjective definition of what a satisfactory relationship means for women.

First of all, it's a goalpost shift since OP is about getting into a relationship and (quite correctly) not about "meaningful" relationships. It's absurd to add that qualifier at all as it renders any objective comparison pointless. 

Secondly, before any relationship can even become "meaningful", it has to start in the first place. I think you know that this is harder for men so you've added a subjective hurdle and exclusively ascribed it to women after the fact in order to somehow balance it out. 

>I mean we could talk about whether or not it would be easier for an average woman to obtain a relationship that an average man would be happy with, but that's not what they want

People in western democracies don't want contaminated drinking water. There are plenty of people in the world who do because it's still preferable to none at all. I'm sure they'd rather have clean water if that was an option to them.

> many women are open in admitting that, in their relationships, they want to feel beautiful and desired, and they want explicit confirmation of this.

Now what do you think would happen if men wanted this too? 

>It would seem that this only makes it harder for women, then, no? More of them "fighting over" fewer quality men?

The difference is that it's by their own free choice. They price themselves out of the market but it's 100% their own decisions that cause that. That's why so many women *perceive* it difficult to find a man: "I'm only dating billionaires. Damn it's hard to find a man these days". Men don't do this or at least nowhere near to that extent. When they say they can't find a woman, they generally mean that literally and not "after I've filtered out the vast majority of women who don't measure up to my standards, none of the supermodels want me. Damn it's hard to find a woman these days". 

And if a man did that, you'd be all over him for expecting too much. Rightfully so.

> Love how you add and emphasize the subjective qualifier to the assertion thereby placing its truth value entirely in the hands of women. I guess men just have to stand there and nod at whatever plights women complain about.

We're comparing the success that men and women have at obtaining relationships. Success is literally defined as achieving the desired outcome, so yes, we have to factor in the inherently subjective definition of what a satisfactory relationship means for women.

I mean we could talk about whether or not it would be easier for an average woman to obtain a relationship that an average man would be happy with, but that's not what they want, so that's not terribly interesting or insightful.

> Also, women still have the option of approaching a man. But men don't have the option of not approaching a woman. [...] That's the price you pay when you place the responsibility of your success into the hands of others. Easy solution: stop doing that.

I think you'll agree that it's quite common for women to express that they find enterprising and confident men attractive. Moreover, many women are open in admitting that, in their relationships, they want to feel beautiful and desired, and they want explicit confirmation of this.

In those situations, placing the responsibility of their success in the hands of men is literally required for success. Having a man confirm his confidence/value as well as their desirability by taking the initiative to start the relationship, be the one to propose, etc.

> And it just so happens that "the most attractive" among men are a far smaller minority than women.

It would seem that this only makes it harder for women, then, no? More of them "fighting over" fewer quality men?

column6:
Except this racial group you have created is so broad that I don't see how you can continue to think there is no social component. You are essentially saying that anyone in the African-European-South American Native  triangle is of the same race.


Also, the white/black/Asian works fine for Europeans, Africans, and East Asians, but what about other groups less often found in the United States?  What race do you categorize Armenians? Turks? Berbers? Maronite Christians? Persians? Balochi? Uzbeks?  Sephardic Jews? Punjabis?

There is nothing biological about putting them in one category or another. You'd need to invent new racial categories for them based on the differences that your particular society considers important enough to merit different categories. 

For example, Indian-Americans are considered under the "Asian" racial category along with Korean/Chinese/Japanese Americans, while they tend to have more genetic/ancestral/biological similarities with Persian-Americans, who are generally considered under the "white" category.

column6:
It seems like you've fallen into the gender vs sex debate.

Sex is biological as you pointed out. Men have male sex organs, females have female sex organs, and a few people are somewhere in between.

Gender is how people are expected to behave in a society. This would be things like men make money and women stay at home. Or women wearing makeup. Or dresses not being appropriate attire for men. None of those things are rooted in biology.

So it is perfectly possible for someone to be a biological male but feels more in line with society's of a women.

"Are you kidding me? You're trying to turn this into a gender debate? This is about racial categories, not about people's personal identities or societal expectations. column6: You aren't, really.  You are perhaps subsidizing med school for people who don't know what they want to do.  You are subsidizing residency in urology, plastic surgery, and perhaps ob/gyn, three fields that are certainly necessary.  They hone those skills on people with ureteral obstructions, car accidents, and cervical cancer.  And then they spend a little time helping people with gender dysphoria.  Whatever money you spend on it is on their services for that day and not for their education/specialization.  Even if one or two make that their life focus (and I highly doubt they have the volume to do that), you and they did not know that would be their focus when they went into med school or residency.

You need ob/gyns.  You need urologists.  You need plastic surgeons.  You cannot have a decent health care system without them.  You can get rid of the transplant surgeons based on your criteria if you like, though I don't necessarily recommend it.  If you have a shortage of cardiologists or cardiac surgeons, by all means try to attract people into those specialties.  Raise their salaries, lower their paperwork, whatever you need.  But I don't think that shutting down specific specialties or procedures is going to have a positive impact.

"I completely disagree with you, @user internal-octree. You're not considering the fact that many people who pursue these fields aren't just'subsidizing' their education, they're doing it because they genuinely want to help others. And as for the volume of patients, that's not the point. The point is that these specialties are essential to our healthcare system. We can't just 'get rid' of them because they're not 'lucrative' or 'efficient'. We need a well-rounded system with a variety of specialists, not just a few 'high-demand' ones. And by the way, what's with the personal attack? I'm just trying to have a respectful conversation."

column6:
He made the assumption that he knew more than she did about something of which he had a passing knowledge. 

He could have said something as simple as, "I'm reading this book about roller derby, and I really enjoy it.  Have you ever seen a roller derby bout?"

By making the assumption that he knew more than her, he lost the opportunity to get a more in-depth look at roller derby than almost any other person.  

Instead, she nodded, put on headphones, and basically ignored him.  

It's a very simple rule.  Try to be INCLUSIVE in your conversation.  Invite the other person to make contributions, because you might get surprised.

column6: In theory, having the morals and sense of a community that religion is supposed to provide is always a net positive. It becomes a negative when the religion becomes a barrier to progress. For example, in many Muslim societies where women are not allowed to work, around half of the potential labor force participants are cut out, which greatly lowers the nation's productivity.

Just the fact that the US has had this separation for centuries is not an argument for the separation of church and state in itself. 

If you consider a best-case scenario of a religion: people sharing ideals and morals and basing their lives and actions around that in a beneficial way, then wouldn't a state based on this religion be a good fit? All the things a state provides for its citizens are compatible with such a religion.

"Are you kidding me? You think the US has been a utopia just because we've managed to separate church and state? That's not a hard and fast rule, and it's been a constant struggle. And as for your 'best-case scenario' of a religion-based state, that's just a pipe dream. The moment you tie a state's policies to a specific faith, you're basically saying that only certain people are worthy of rights and freedoms. Newsflash: that's not democracy, that's theocracy. And we all know how that ends."

"Let's keep the discussion respectful and fact-based. @mechanical-fugue, your points are valid, but let's avoid personal attacks. @boolean-pumice, your hypothetical scenario is interesting, but remember that diversity of thought is crucial in a democratic society. column6:
> no, if they have no legal responsibility for it

> lol no, this is your implication. That sentence is totally unfounded and baseless assumption.

If a biological parent is allowed to divest themself of their child, giving them no legal responsibility for it, then if both parents do this the child, unless taken by someone else (which the parents, not being legally bound, would have no obligation to ensure), will starve.

So again, yes or no? And again, anything other than yes or no will terminate the discussion.  

> This kind of argument is called a fallacy.

Not letting you reframe other people's arguments away from their original intention so they're more agreeable to you is not a fallacy. You will answer the questions put to you as they are, not as you would prefer they be.

>Yeah, and I'm the one who asked the blasted question, so the only authority that matters is mine. Any other interpretation of my words than the one I'm giving you is incorrect, full-stop. You have not answered my questions, only reframed them such that you could avoid answering them, or focused on tangents meaningless to the actual point.

This kind of argument is called a fallacy. You presented an already biased question and expect people to answer it according to your view? I for one will not fall for that trap.

>is the wellbeing of the child more or less important than the autonomy of the biological parents?

no, **if they have no legal responsibility for it**

>Keeping in mind that "no" would imply it is acceptable for both parents to leave their child to starve.

lol no, this is *your* implication. That sentence is totally unfounded and baseless assumption.

column6:
That site has a *heavy* conservative slant, which is fine by me as long as they make compelling arguments. Unfortunately, they don't seem to do that.

>California has enacted several laws that heavily restrict such vital fire-preventing measures as logging, removal of dead trees, and clearing of dry underbrush.

No mention of specific laws. I can believe "logging", but I was unable to find laws that prohibit removal of dead trees and clearing of underbrush. If you know of any, I'd like to see them. In 2005, CA passed a law that extended the defensible space clearance around homes and structures (where homeowners are required to remove underbrush/wildfire fuel) from 30 feet to 100 feet.

>It used to be they'd clear out some of the dead underbrush, but a law passed and it considered that tampering with the environment (or something)

Do you know what the specific law was? I'm genuinely interested.

>When a 2013 environmental impact report advocated the benefits of large-scale vegetation management in San Diego County, activists violently rejected its conclusions.

Yes, and...? As far as I can tell, this activism did not result in a change to fire safety/underbrush clearing legislation. Environmental activists have a history of acting up; this doesn't mean that they get to rewrite the laws.

>Worse still, government agencies have actively stymied rescue efforts.  In this case, it was CAL FIRE withholding a license for a Boeing 747 Global SuperTanker firefighting plane

This just seems flat-out wrong. The snippet includes a link to the following article:

[http://www.capradio.org/99314](http://www.capradio.org/99314)

Which says nothing about Cal Fire intentionally withholding any licenses. It says that the process for certifying the 747 was in progress, and as far as I can tell all parties (both Cal Fire and the company that owns the plane) were interested in getting it certified and in the air. These days, Cal Fire seems quite happy to have a 747 in their arsenal; I see no reason why they would intentionally delay its certification.

>Top-of-the-line equipment like the SuperTanker is heavily reliant on functioning fixed-base operators (FBOs) for refueling and replenishing payloads.  In the midst of the fires, a campaign to impose price regulations on these very FBOs emerged, threatening to undermine the first line of defense in providing essential services during natural disasters.

Again, if the authors actually took the time to read the source they're citing, they would realize that it does not support their argument. This campaign targeted three specific FBOs at three specific airports, **none of which are in California**. One was in Illinois, one in North Carolina, and one was in Florida. This seems to fall under the general category of "i think regulation is bad" rather than being related in any way to California wildfires.

>more regulation certainly isn't what's needed in times of emergency, especially not if these regulations seek to counteract the market forces necessary for rescue efforts to continue their operations.

I agree! But...in this specific case, regulation would likely make rescue/firefighting operations cheaper, by putting a cap on how much FBOs can charge for services.

>Oregon, a state equally prone to wildfires, has successfully prevented the outbreak of large-scale fires since it implemented comprehensive thinning and pruning measures in the 1990s.

This is blatantly false. 4 of the 5 worst wildfires since 1900 happened *after 2010*.

[https://www.oregonlive.com/expo/news/erry-2018/08/ef82ada98f1307/oregons-5-worst-wildfires-sinc.html](https://www.oregonlive.com/expo/news/erry-2018/08/ef82ada98f1307/oregons-5-worst-wildfires-sinc.html)

&#x200B;

While reading this article, I went from "ok, that kind of makes sense" to "wait, is that really true", to "what the fuck, now they're just flat-out lying". I would like to take this opportunity to remind everyone that just because an article has links to sources, this is not proof that any of the sources support the author's claims.

edit: I would also like to take this opportunity to say: Climate change is real, and it's happening right now. People are dying because of it. Get with the program.

column6:
The idea behind our criminal justice system is to punish and rehabilitate. The punishment is there to discourage anyone from committing crimes. The rehabilitation is to help assure they don't commit anymore crimes.

The facts are: women commit less crimes, commit less violent crimes, and recidivate less. Those are facts not up for discussion. Criminal sentencing does have guidelines, and except for some very misguided policies like the three strikes laws, judges have leeway in how they sentence the convicted. It's up to their *judge*ment.

Now you can whine and be all like "But that's not fair." And then realize life isn't fair. We don't punish in proportion to other crimes. Each case is judged on its merits. With the goal of rehabilitation in mind, if a sentence of two years for woman committing the same crime as a man that received a five years sentence discourages each of them enough from committing anymore crime, then the sentence has done its job.

Basically, for the same reason a first-time offender can get a lighter sentence than a multiple offender, judges have the discretion to assign sentences based on how likely the person is to commit future crimes, or not.

It is well-established that men and women get vastly different sentences for the same crime. So most of your argument is hogwash.

But you are also arguing that judge's should sentence based upon sexually profiling informed by stats. Fuck that.
column18: ['Busy', 'Moderate', 'Meticulous attention to detail, habit of double-checking everything']

column6: "Are you kidding me, calm-sycamore? You think you're so smart with your'sexually profiling' nonsense. Newsflash: the stats are there, and they're not just some made-up numbers. And yeah, maybe judges should take into account the fact that women are less likely to commit violent crimes and recidivate. It's not about'sexually profiling', it's about common sense. You're just too dense to see it. And by the way, what's with the 'fuck that' at the end of your post? Trying to sound edgy and cool? Please, you're just a try-hard."
